message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Modern neural/AI TTS systems are designed to sound human, natural, and conversational, but this naturalness comes at the expense of accuracy/intelligibility -- a core argument for why power listeners reject them."

**Source:** https://github.com/orgs/espeak-ng/discussions/2338 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "These modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim skeptically. Let me start by examining the primary source and searching for contradicting evidence.

input.prompt: Find the exact quote about modern neural/AI TTS systems being designed to sound human, natural, conversational, and this coming at the expense of accuracy. Who said it? What is the full context? Is this from a blind/screen-reader power user? Quote the surrounding paragraphs verbatim.

input.query: blind screen reader users prefer formant synthesis Eloquence DECtalk over neural TTS intelligibility high speed

content.content:
I cannot find the quote you're looking for in the provided web page content. 

The page shows only a GitHub discussion post by fabiolimace from January 14, 2026, which references an external article titled "The State of Modern AI Text To Speech Systems for Screen Reader Users" by Samuel Proulx (published January 5, 2026). However, the actual content of that article is not included in the webpage excerpt provided—only a link to it at `https://stuff.interfree.ca/2026/01/05/ai-tts-for-screenreaders.html`.

To locate the specific quote about neural/AI TTS systems prioritizing naturalness over accuracy, you would need to access the original article directly through that URL.

Web search results for query: "blind screen reader users prefer formant synthesis Eloquence DECtalk over neural TTS intelligibility high speed"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) On the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech for individuals with early-onset blindness","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228440880_On_the_intelligibility_of_fast_synthesized_speech_for_individuals_with_early-onset_blindness"},{"title":"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech-blind vs.\" normally hearing\" persons | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228846770_Comprehension_of_ultra-fast_speech-blind_vs_normally_hearing_persons"},{"title":"Speech Synthesizers | American Foundation for the Blind","url":"https://afb.org/node/16207/speech-synthesizers"},{"title":"A Unit Selection Text-to-Speech Synthesis System Optimized for Use with Screen Readers | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/224184121_A_Unit_Selection_Text-to-Speech_Synthesis_System_Optimized_for_Use_with_Screen_Readers"},{"title":"Vocal effort modeling in neural TTS for improving the intelligibility of   synthetic speech in noise","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.10637"},{"title":"On the intelligibility of fast synthesized speech for people who are blind: A cross‐system comparison. | The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | AIP Publishing","url":"https://pubs.aip.org/asa/jasa/article/129/4_Supplement/2421/619620/On-the-intelligibility-of-fast-synthesized-speech"},{"title":"Macedonian Speech Synthesis for Assistive Technology Applications","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2205.09198"},{"title":"Interactive TTS optimization tool","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/8352270"}]

Based on the web search results, I found relevant information about screen reader users and text-to-speech synthesis preferences:

People who are blind typically prefer to listen to synthesized speech at speeds multiple times real time, and consequently develop strong preferences for particular text-to-speech engines and voices. However, these usage-based preferences may lead to potentially incorrect assumptions about which TTS approaches are "best" for fast speech.

The research examined various synthesis approaches. Formant-synthesized speech can be reliably intelligible, even at very high speeds, avoiding the acoustic glitches that are commonly found in concatenative systems. 

When comparing fast synthesized speech to linearly time-compressed natural speech, researchers found that intelligibility of fast synthesized speech was higher than that of linearly time compressed natural speech for blind listeners.

The search results indicate that researchers studied male and female voices from multiple TTS engines representing the main approaches to TTS: diphone synthesis, unit selection synthesis, HMM-based synthesis, and formant synthesis. This represents a comprehensive comparison including formant-based systems like DECtalk and Eloquence alongside newer approaches.

input.prompt: Find the exact quote: "These modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy." Quote the full surrounding context verbatim. Who is the author (are they a blind/screen-reader power user)? What specific accuracy problems do they describe? Do they say naturalness CAUSES the inaccuracy, or just that both happen to be true? Do they distinguish intelligibility from pronunciation accuracy?

input.query: neural TTS naturalness intelligibility tradeoff "clear speech" hyperarticulation Lombard improve intelligibility not mutually exclusive

content.content:
# Analysis of the Text-to-Speech Article

**Full Quote with Context:**
"These modern systems are developed to sound human, natural, and conversational. Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy. In my testing, both models had a tendency to skip words, read numbers incorrectly, chop off short utterances, and ignore prosody hints from text punctuation."

**Author:** Samuel Proulx, who identifies as a screen reader user and describes himself as part of the blind power-user community.

**Specific Accuracy Problems Identified:**
- Skipping words
- Reading numbers incorrectly
- Chopping off short utterances
- Ignoring prosody hints from punctuation

**Causality Claim:** The author uses "Unfortunately this seems to come at the expense of accuracy," suggesting naturalness prioritization correlates with reduced accuracy, but stops short of claiming direct causation. The phrasing indicates trade-offs rather than definitive cause-and-effect.

**Intelligibility vs. Pronunciation:** The author does not explicitly distinguish between these concepts. The errors described (skipped words, incorrect numbers) blend both intelligibility and pronunciation concerns without separating them analytically.

Web search results for query: "neural TTS naturalness intelligibility tradeoff "clear speech" hyperarticulation Lombard improve intelligibility not mutually exclusive"

Links: [{"title":"(PDF) Perception of Concatenative vs. Neural Text-To-Speech (TTS): Differences in Intelligibility in Noise and Language Attitudes","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/344893836_Perception_of_Concatenative_vs_Neural_Text-To-Speech_TTS_Differences_in_Intelligibility_in_Noise_and_Language_Attitudes"},{"title":"LOMBARD SPEECH SYNTHESIS FOR ANY VOICE WITH CONTROLLABLE STYLE EMBEDDINGS","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.12966"},{"title":"Vocal effort modeling in neural TTS for improving the intelligibility of   synthetic speech in noise","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.10637"},{"title":"Enhancing Speech Intelligibility in Text-To-Speech Synthesis using Speaking","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.05809"},{"title":"Whispered and Lombard Neural Speech Synthesis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.05313"},{"title":"(PDF) The Lombard Effect: a physiological reflex or a controlled intelligibility enhancement?","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/45401886_The_Lombard_Effect_a_physiological_reflex_or_a_controlled_intelligibility_enhancement"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding neural TTS, naturalness, intelligibility, and related concepts:

## Naturalness vs. Intelligibility in Neural TTS

Research has found that while listeners rated neural TTS methods as sounding more naturalistic, they performed better in concatenative TTS conditions, suggesting a potential tradeoff between naturalness and intelligibility in speech synthesis.

## Lombard Effect and Hyperarticulation

To improve speech intelligibility in the presence of noise, humans adapt their voice through the Lombard effect by increasing the intensity of the signal and modifying voice characteristics as a result of increased vocal effort. In noise, voice intensity increases, spectral characteristics of speech shift to the high-frequency region, and the amplitude and speed of articulatory movements increase (hyperarticulation).

## Improving Intelligibility Without Sacrificing Naturalness

Recent research suggests these goals may not be mutually exclusive: A controllable Lombard TTS system preserves naturalness and speaker identity while enhancing intelligibility under noise. Additionally, in cross-lingual settings, a style-controllable TTS substantially outperformed baselines in both intelligibility and naturalness, demonstrating that improvements in these dimensions can be achieved simultaneously.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I now have the exact quote and context. Let me probe the key tension: the claim says naturalness "comes at the expense of accuracy/intelligibility," but the source quote describes accuracy problems (word skipping, number errors, dropped utterances) that are model robustness/reliability failures, not necessarily intelligibility-of-clear-speech failures. Let me check whether the literature supports the specific framing of the claim and whether power listeners reject neural TTS specifically due to a naturalness-intelligibility tradeoff.

input.query: neural TTS hallucination word skipping deletion attention failures autoregressive reliability robustness errors

Does it attribute lower intelligibility to naturalness/conversational design specifically, or to other factors? Does it support or contradict the claim that naturalness comes at the expense of intelligibility?

Web search results for query: "neural TTS hallucination word skipping deletion attention failures autoregressive reliability robustness errors"

Links: [{"title":"[2011.00935] FeatherTTS: Robust and Efficient attention based Neural TTS","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.00935"},{"title":"Addressing Hallucinations in Speech Synthesis LLMs with the NVIDIA NeMo T5-TTS Model | NVIDIA Technical Blog","url":"https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/addressing-hallucinations-in-speech-synthesis-llms-with-the-nvidia-nemo-t5-tts-model/"},{"title":"FeatherTTS: Robust and Efficient attention based Neural TTS","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2011.00935"},{"title":"A Survey on Neural Speech Synthesis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.15561"},{"title":"Non-Attentive Tacotron: Robust and Controllable Neural TTS Synthesis   Including Unsupervised Duration Modeling","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.04301"},{"title":"(PDF) Eliminating stability hallucinations in llm-based tts models via attention guidance","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/395806264_Eliminating_stability_hallucinations_in_llm-based_tts_models_via_attention_guidance"},{"title":"VRAIN-UPV MLLP's system for the Blizzard Challenge 2021","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2110.15792"},{"title":"TalkNet 2: Non-Autoregressive Depth-Wise Separable Convolutional Model   for Speech Synthesis with Explicit Pitch and Duration Prediction","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2104.08189"},{"title":"TalkNet: Fully-Convolutional Non-Autoregressive Speech Synthesis Model","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.05514"},{"title":"FastSpeech: Fast, Robust and Controllable Text to Speech - Speech Research","url":"https://speechresearch.github.io/fastspeech/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what researchers have found about neural TTS reliability issues:

## Common Robustness Problems

In neural TTS, robust issues such as word skipping, repeating, and attention collapse often happen in acoustic models when generating mel-spectrogram sequence from character/phoneme sequence.

## Root Causes

The causes of these robust issues are from two categories: 1) The difficulty in learning the alignments between characters/phonemes and mel-spectrograms; 2) The exposure bias and error propagation problems incurred in autoregressive generation.

More specifically, autoregressive networks that predict the output one frame at a time are trained to decide whether to stop at each frame, and thus a misprediction on a single frame can result in serious failures such as early cut-off. Meanwhile, there are little to no hard constraints imposed on the attention mechanism to prevent problems such as repetition, skipping, long pause or babbling.

## Hallucinations in LLM-Based TTS

Hallucination in TTS occurs when the generated speech deviates from the intended text, causing errors ranging from minor mispronunciations to entirely incorrect words. Existing methods like VALL-E, which condition acoustic token generation on text transcriptions, often suffer from content inaccuracies and elevated word error rates due to semantic misinterpretations of acoustic tokens, resulting in word skipping and errors.

## Solutions

Researchers have proposed several approaches, including non-autoregressive models, attention mechanisms with monotonic constraints, and improved alignment techniques to address these reliability issues.

content.text:
I have enough to assess this carefully. Let me weigh the evidence.

The claim has two parts: (A) modern neural TTS is designed to sound human/natural/conversational, and (B) "this naturalness comes at the expense of accuracy/intelligibility."

Key findings:

1. **The quote IS accurately reproduced** and comes from a genuine blind power-user (Samuel Proulx) — a credible primary voice from the screen-reader community. The source quality is fine for a community-knowledge claim.

2. **But there's a significant overreach/conflation problem.** The author's "accuracy" failures are: skipping words, misreading numbers, chopping off short utterances, ignoring punctuation prosody. The robustness literature confirms these are **autoregressive attention/alignment failures and hallucinations** — a model *reliability/robustness* problem, NOT the acoustic-phonetic *intelligibility-at-speed* problem the research question is about. The claim conflates "accuracy" (reading the right words) with "intelligibility" (clarity of the acoustic signal when sped up). These are distinct.

3. **The causal framing ("this naturalness comes at the expense of") is the author's hedged inference** ("seems to come at the expense of"), not an established mechanism. The robustness literature attributes word-skipping to autoregressive exposure bias and weak attention constraints — not to naturalness/conversational design. Word skipping happens for architectural reasons, and is being fixed (non-autoregressive models, NeMo T5-TTS anti-hallucination) without sacrificing naturalness.

4. **Contradicting evidence on the intelligibility tradeoff exists.** Recent peer-reviewed work (controllable Lombard TTS, vocal-effort modeling, style-controllable TTS) demonstrates neural systems can improve intelligibility *while preserving* naturalness — i.e., the two are NOT inherently in opposition. So the strong universal framing ("naturalness comes at the expense of intelligibility") is contradicted.

5. **There IS a real, evidence-backed kernel:** the Cabral/JASA-type cross-system studies do find concatenative/formant beats neural on fast-speech intelligibility, and one study found neural rated more natural but concatenative more intelligible. So the *softer* version of the claim has support.

The claim as written overstates a hedged community observation into a "core argument" with a causal mechanism, and conflates word-level accuracy (a robustness artifact) with acoustic intelligibility (the research question's actual concern). The supporting quote does not establish causation, does not mention intelligibility-at-speed, and the broader literature shows naturalness and intelligibility are not inherently traded off.

input.evidence: The quote is reproduced accurately and comes from a credible primary source (Samuel Proulx, a blind screen-reader power user, "The State of Modern AI TTS for Screen Reader Users", 2026-01-05, linked from espeak-ng discussion #2338). But the CLAIM overreaches the quote in two ways. (1) CONFLATION: Proulx's "accuracy" failures are skipping words, misreading numbers, chopping off short utterances, and ignoring punctuation prosody. The robustness literature (Survey on Neural Speech Synthesis arXiv:2106.15561; NVIDIA NeMo T5-TTS anti-hallucination blog) attributes these to autoregressive exposure bias, error propagation, and weak attention/alignment constraints — i.e. model RELIABILITY/hallucination artifacts, NOT the acoustic-phonetic INTELLIGIBILITY-at-speed that the research question concerns. The claim wrongly merges "accuracy" (reading the correct words) with "intelligibility" (signal clarity when sped up). (2) CAUSATION OVERREACH: Proulx hedges — "this SEEMS to come at the expense of accuracy" — yet the claim restates it as a definitive mechanism ("this naturalness comes at the expense"). No source establishes that conversational/natural design CAUSES word-skipping; the literature blames architecture, and the fixes (non-autoregressive models, monotonic attention, T5-TTS) reduce errors without sacrificing naturalness. (3) DIRECT CONTRADICTION: peer-reviewed work shows naturalness and intelligibility are NOT inherently traded off — controllable Lombard TTS (arXiv:2601.12966) and vocal-effort modeling (arXiv:2203.10637) improve intelligibility WHILE preserving naturalness and speaker identity. A softer version of the claim has support (a concatenative-vs-neural study found neural rated more natural but concatenative more intelligible-in-noise; JASA fast-speech cross-system studies favor formant/concatenative at high speed), but the strong universal causal framing as a "core" reason is unsupported by the cited quote and partially contradicted by current literature.
input.counterSource: arXiv:2601.12966 (controllable Lombard neural TTS preserves naturalness while boosting intelligibility); arXiv:2203.10637 (vocal-effort neural TTS improves intelligibility without losing naturalness); arXiv:2106.15561 Survey on Neural Speech Synthesis and NVIDIA NeMo T5-TTS blog (word-skipping/number errors are autoregressive attention/hallucination failures, not a naturalness tradeoff)

content.text: I've completed the adversarial verification and returned my structured output. The claim is refuted (medium confidence): while the quote is accurately reproduced from a credible primary source, the claim overreaches it by (1) conflating word-level "accuracy"/robustness failures with acoustic "intelligibility," (2) restating the author's hedged "seems to" inference as a definitive causal mechanism, and (3) being partially contradicted by peer-reviewed work showing neural TTS can improve intelligibility while preserving naturalness.
